commands.repair_reason: structured ClawSweeper needs-human verdict with repairable P-severity findings (sha=037b210e5136c4bfa6c24f6b4a456d2c49692fae)
commands.review_summary:
The PR adds optional `meta` support to gateway response frames, forwards existing response metadata from the WebSocket responder, updates generated Swift protocol models, adds schema validation tests, and documents the field.

PR surface: Source +1, Tests +27, Docs +9, Other +4. Total +41 across 5 files.

Reproducibility: yes. for source-level reproduction: current main accepts response metadata and some handlers populate it, but the WebSocket response frame and schema omit it. I did not run a live gateway/client reproduction in this read-only review.
commands.review_followup: - [P1] Needs contributor or maintainer follow-up for the Swift compatibility finding and real behavior proof; ClawSweeper should not queue repair while proof remains a contributor gate.

target.title: fix: forward response-frame meta to clients
target.body:
Closes #98203

## What Problem This Solves

Clients connected to the gateway cannot read per-response metadata. The `respond(ok, payload, error, meta)` callback in `message-handler.ts` accepts a `meta` argument, and the agent method handlers already populate it — `server-methods/agent.ts` returns `{ cached: true, runId }` when it serves a deduplicated request, and passes `{ runId }` / `{ aborted }` elsewhere — but the `res` frame is built as `{ type, id, ok, payload, error }` and `meta` is forwarded only to `logWs`. Combined with `ResponseFrameSchema` being `additionalProperties: false` with no `meta` key, the metadata the gateway computes never reaches the wire. A transport bridge reading `res.meta?.cached` to suppress retries on an already-cached failure always sees `undefined` and re-sends it.

## Why This Change Was Made

This wires up an already-built-but-dropped channel rather than adding a new feature. Two minimal changes:

- `packages/gateway-protocol/src/schema/frames.ts` — add `meta: Type.Optional(Type.Record(Type.String(), Type.Unknown()))` to `ResponseFrameSchema` (same idiom already used for `args`/`data` records elsewhere in the package), keeping `additionalProperties: false`.
- `src/gateway/server/ws-connection/message-handler.ts` — spread the existing `meta` onto the `res` frame: `send({ type: "res", id: req.id, ok, payload, error, ...(meta ? { meta } : {}) })`. `send` is typed `(obj: unknown) => void`, so the spread is type-safe, and the frame stays backward compatible when no handler supplies `meta`.

Non-goal: this PR does not change which handlers populate `meta` or introduce new metadata fields — it only makes the existing `meta` observable to clients.

## User Impact

Clients can now read response metadata (e.g. `cached`) from `res` frames, so transports/bridges can stop retrying responses the gateway already cached or deduplicated. No behavior change for clients that ignore `meta`; frames without `meta` are unchanged.

## Evidence

- Added `validateResponseFrame` tests in `packages/gateway-protocol/src/index.test.ts`: a frame without `meta` validates (backward compatible), a frame carrying `{ cached, runId }` validates, and an unknown top-level field is still rejected.
- Validated the schema change through the repo's real `typebox` `Compile` path locally (mirroring `lazyCompile`/`validateResponseFrame`): 4/4 — meta-less valid, meta valid, unknown field rejected, non-object `meta` rejected.
- A full `pnpm install` could not complete in my sandbox (the npm mirror available there repeatedly timed out on a handful of tarballs), so I could not run the full `pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test` lane locally; CI on this PR exercises it. Happy to act on any CI/reviewer findings.

---
🤖 AI-assisted (authored with Claude).
target.author: petroff
